questions.question: Explain function overloading and state its type of polymorphism with justification.
keyPoints: Definition: Multiple functions with same name but different parameters
Type: Compile-time/Static/Early binding polymorphism
Justification: Decision made at compile time based on function signature

questions.question: What is function signature? List the components that help in differentiating overloaded functions.
keyPoints: Function signature: number, type, and order of parameters
Return type is NOT part of signature
Components: parameter count, parameter types, parameter order

questions.question: Differentiate between function overloading and function overriding.
keyPoints: Overloading: same class, compile-time, based on parameters
Overriding: different classes (base/derived), runtime, virtual functions
Overloading increases readability; overriding implements inheritance/polymorphism

questions.question: What is ambiguity in function overloading? How can it be avoided?
keyPoints: Ambiguity: compiler cannot determine which function to call
Causes: multiple equally good matches, default arguments, type conversions
Avoid by: explicit type casting, unique signatures, avoiding default args with overloads

questions.question: Write a C++ program to overload area() function to calculate area of circle, rectangle, and triangle.
keyPoints: area(float r) - circle: πr²
area(float l, float w) - rectangle: l×w
area(float b, float h, bool) - triangle: ½bh
Demonstrate function calls with different arguments

questions.question: Explain type promotion in function overloading with an example.
keyPoints: Type promotion: automatic conversion to match function
Example: char→int, float→double, short→int
Compiler selects function requiring least conversion
Exact match has highest priority

questions.question: Can overloaded functions differ only in return type? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: No, return type is NOT part of function signature
Overloading is based only on parameter list
Same name + same parameters = compilation error regardless of return type
This rule ensures compiler can resolve calls at compile time

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating function overloading with add() function to add two integers, two floats, and three integers. Also explain the concept of function signature and how the compiler resolves the calls.
keyPoints: Program with: add(int,int), add(float,float), add(int,int,int)
Each function returns appropriate type (int/float)
Demonstrate calls: add(5,10), add(3.5f,2.5f), add(1,2,3)
Explain: compiler checks argument types and matches signature
Exact match > promotion > conversion > user-defined

questions.question: Explain the concept of compile-time polymorphism. How is function overloading an example of compile-time polymorphism? What are its advantages?
keyPoints: Compile-time/Static polymorphism: resolved during compilation
Function overloading: compiler decides which function to call based on arguments
Advantages: improved readability, reusability, type safety, faster execution (no runtime overhead)
Code example demonstrating compile-time decision

questions.question: What is function hiding? How does it differ from function overloading? Explain with a code example involving base and derived classes.
keyPoints: Function hiding: derived class function with same name hides ALL base class versions
Different from overloading: overloading is in same scope, hiding across inheritance
Code example showing base class overloaded functions and derived class non-virtual function
To access hidden functions: use BaseClass::functionName()

questions.question: Write a C++ program with an overloaded volume() function to calculate volume of cube, cylinder, and sphere. Handle the ambiguity that may arise if default arguments are used with overloading.
keyPoints: volume(float side) - cube: side³
volume(float radius, float height) - cylinder: πr²h
volume(float radius) - sphere: 4/3πr³
Demonstrate calls with appropriate arguments
Show ambiguity scenario and how to resolve it
